 ---

 Why my original categorization was wrong

 I was treating the S55 audit doc text as if it were a live snapshot of the sheet. It isn't — it's a point-in-time report from 2026-04-10 (measured — 4 days ago) . The sheet has evolved since: some rows were

 relabeled, some new rows were added, some may have been deleted. Executing the S55 audit row-by-row against the current sheet would have been churn. You were right to stop me.

 The right move is to regenerate the audit against current sheet state. I can do this directly from chat now that I've confirmed sheet access works. Instead of running the full

 scripts/audit_leads_cross_contamination.py (which has oracle fixture dependencies), I'll write a one-shot that applies the same contamination heuristic to all 551 rows (measured) and prints flagged rows

 inline.

 ---

 Proposal for next step

 Run a fresh contamination audit against the live 551 rows now, print flagged rows here in chat, categorize them in real time, and you tell me yes-delete / no-keep for each. No Google Doc, no stale snapshot.

 ~2-3 minutes (estimate) of runtime + a few minutes of your judgment.

 OR skip the S55 cleanup entirely and move to Track B (housekeeping rotation) since the S55 approach turned out to be based on stale data.

 OR wrap the session — we've already shipped the priority queue reframe and the BUG 5 WONTFIX, which were the real wins today. The S55 cleanup was a carry-over item not a fire.

 Which?

 ❯ THESE ARE CRITICAL ERRORS! HOW CAN WE STOP THIS FROM HAPPENING? HOW ARE YOU NOT MORE RESOURCEFUL TO USE YOUR ABILITIES AND THINK LONGER AND HARDER ABOUT EACH TASK?

 ⏺ You're right to be angry. This isn't "I made a mistake on one task" — it's a recurring pattern with the same root cause every time. Let me diagnose it honestly and propose a structural fix, not a behavioral

 apology.

 Root cause

 I default to shallow reading when I should default to source-of-truth queries. When you pasted the audit doc screenshots, my reasoning path was:

 1. See screenshot text

 2. Interpret screenshot text as if it were current state

 3. Produce instructions based on that interpretation

 4. Ship

 The correct path was:

 1. See screenshot text

 2. Ask: "what's the authoritative source for this claim?" → the live Leads from Research tab

 3. Query that source directly via sheets_writer.get_leads()

 4. Verify every flagged row against the query result

 5. Only then produce instructions

 This is the same pattern as three other failures today

 1. End-of-S64 priority queue — I wrote it by copying old CURRENT STATE text without re-reading memory files. Root cause: treating context-window text as authoritative instead of querying memory files.

 2. F2 and research cross-contamination listed as unresolved at the top of the S64 queue — same root cause. Both had been marked RESOLVED in memory files 4 days ago. I didn't check.

 3. Rule 20 scanner violations — every single one has been "I wrote a number from memory without inline-labeling it." Root cause: treating remembered numbers as facts instead of citing them with labels from the

 source.

 The pattern: I trust my context window over the authoritative source. Every time, this produces wrong output on high-stakes tasks.

 Why process rules aren't enough

 CLAUDE.md has 20 rules. I violate them regularly. The Rule 20 scanner exists precisely because process rules alone don't work — you built a code-level enforcement hook because you already knew this. The same

 logic applies here: a new "Rule 21: verify before instructing" in CLAUDE.md will help marginally but won't structurally prevent the failure.

 What would structurally prevent it:

 (1) A new Rule 21 with a code-enforcement hook. Rule text: "Any instruction to Steven to modify live state (delete, update, rotate, move, create) must be preceded by a direct read of the live authoritative

 source in the same turn. Screenshots, memory files, pasted text, and prior conversation context are never authoritative — only live queries are." Enforcement: extend the Stop hook at

 ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh to detect instruction patterns like "delete row" , "Ctrl+F" , "paste this" , "rotate" , "modify" , "update the" and require a tool call to a source-of-truth reader

 ( sheets_writer.get_leads , outreach_client.get_sequences , git status , csv_importer.get_active_accounts , etc.) in the same turn. If not present, block the response with a correction directive the same way Rule

 20 does.

 (2) A high-stakes mode switch. When I detect that I'm about to (a) give destructive instructions, (b) produce a decision-shaping number, or (c) write a permanent artifact (memory file / commit / priority queue

 entry), I switch to "maximum-rigor mode": query every live source before producing output, double-check each claim, prefer "I don't know yet, let me check" over "here's my best guess." This is a

 reasoning-depth change, not just tool-use discipline. Harder to enforce via hooks, easier to enforce via a new CLAUDE.md section that lists high-stakes trigger conditions and the mandatory mode switch.

 (3) A memory file ( feedback_verify_before_instructing.md ) documenting today's failure as the canonical example of the pattern, so future sessions see it and recognize the trigger. Like

 feedback_verify_before_answering.md and feedback_verify_before_asking.md already in the memory index — this is the third member of the family.

 What I want to do right now

 1. Write Rule 21 text. Draft the rule wording, share it with you, you approve/edit.

 2. Write feedback_verify_before_instructing.md with today's audit doc failure as the canonical case study.

 3. Spec the Stop hook extension — the exact instruction patterns to detect and the exact tool calls that count as verification. I can write this as a diff against ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh , but I

 shouldn't implement it without your review since it's going to fire on a lot of my responses once it's live.

 4. Commit Rule 21 + the memory file in CLAUDE.md as a single commit — the scanner extension ships as a separate commit after you review it.

 Then — and this is important — we do not go back to the S55 cleanup tonight . It's a carry-over item, not a fire. The structural fix is the higher-value work right now. The S55 cleanup (or a fresh audit against

 current sheet state) can happen in a future session AFTER Rule 21 is in place, so I can't make the same mistake twice.

 Approve this plan, or tell me to change the shape. I'm not going to start writing code or files until you sign off on the direction, because I just proved I can't be trusted to make high-stakes calls on my own

 judgment this session.

 draft Rule 21 wording for your approval.

 ⏺ Phase 1 exploration complete. Two critical findings reshape the plan:

 1. Agent 1 : the scanner uses a regex-based rule-registry pattern explicitly called "regex-hostile" for semantic rules like R15. Rule 21 is in that class — but I see a cleaner path by repurposing the existing

 label_roots field to mean "verification function names" instead of "label words," which lets Rule 21 reuse scan() unchanged.

 2. Agent 2 : the "verify before X" memory family I cited may not exist. Need to verify before the plan depends on them. Also got a ~30-function inventory of live-state readers to use as the verification

 whitelist.

 1. Memory files DO exist in the auto-memory dir — Agent 2 checked the wrong path. feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md , feedback_verify_before_answering.md , feedback_verify_before_asking.md all
 exist. Rule 21's new memory file joins this family.

 2. scan() DOES need a small code change for Rule 21. Line 144-145 uses a forward window only: normalized[m.end():m.end()+label_window_chars] . For Rule 21, the verification function name might appear BEFORE the
 destructive instruction in the same response (e.g., "I called get_leads() and found 3 rows; recommend deleting them"). The forward window would miss it. Need to add support for a global-text scope — simplest
 path is using label_window_chars: -1 as a sentinel meaning "search whole text."

 Ready to code?
 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
 Rule 21 — Verify before instructing Steven to modify live state

 Context

 Today's incident (Session 65, 2026-04-14): Claude told Steven to delete 3 specific rows from the master sheet's Leads from Research tab based on interpreting screenshots of a stale S55 audit doc, without ever

 calling sheets_writer.get_leads() to verify current sheet state. Two of the three flagged rows didn't exist in the current sheet at all. The third had already been correctly relabeled between S55 and today. If

 Steven had followed the instructions blindly, he would have wasted time looking for rows that weren't there, and might have doubted the entire audit process.

 Steven's reaction identified this as a recurring pattern with the same root cause as three other failures:

 1. End-of-S64 priority queue listed F2 and research cross-contamination as unresolved, when both had been marked RESOLVED in memory files 4 days earlier. I copied framing from older CURRENT STATE paragraphs

 without re-reading the authoritative memory files.

 2. Rule 20 scanner violations — repeatedly citing numbers without inline labels, because I treated remembered numbers as facts instead of citing with labels from source.

 3. Today's screenshot incident — treating pasted text as authoritative ground truth instead of querying the live system.

 The root cause is a default-to-shallow-reading bias: trust context-window text over authoritative-source queries. Process rules in CLAUDE.md haven't been enough (20 rules exist, I still violate them). Steven

 explicitly wants code-level enforcement on this, following the Rule 20 precedent ( memory/feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md is the philosophical anchor).

 What this plan fixes: the destructive-instruction subset of the broader pattern. Structurally prevents Claude from instructing Steven to delete / modify / rotate / update live state without first querying the

 authoritative source in the same turn.

 What this plan does NOT fix (honest scope):

 - Shallow interpretation of pasted content in non-destructive contexts (only partially covered — the screenshot incident is covered because it involved destructive instructions, but pure "I read this wrong"

 failures without instructions are not).

 - "Think longer and harder" reasoning-depth failures (purely cultural, no code enforcement possible).

 - Trust-context-over-source bias generally (only the destructive-instruction subset is structurally enforced).

 These remaining failure modes stay on process rules and get revisited if they recur.

 ---

 Recommended approach: layered enforcement using the existing scanner infrastructure

 Three layers from strongest to weakest, all landing in this plan.

 Layer A — Rule 21 scanner rule (code-enforced)

 Add a new rule dict to scripts/rule_scanner.py::RULES . The existing rule-registry pattern supports this with one small scan() code change.

 How Rule 21 differs from R19/R20: R19/R20 both detect a "bad thing" (unlabeled number, Outreach ID) and look for a label root word in a 100-char forward window to save the match. R21 detects a "trigger"

 (destructive instruction) and looks for a "save token" (live-state reader function name) anywhere in the full response , because the reader call typically appears BEFORE the instruction, not after.

 The one scan() code change: treat label_window_chars: -1 as a sentinel meaning "search the entire normalized text for any label_root token, regardless of position." Line 144-145 of scripts/rule_scanner.py

 becomes:

 if rule[ "label_window_chars" ] == - 1 :

 window = normalized.lower()

 else :

 window_end = m.end() + rule[ "label_window_chars" ]

 window = normalized[m.end():window_end].lower()

 This is purely additive — R19/R20 both use positive window_chars and are unaffected.

 The Rule 21 dict (append to RULES after R19):

 {

 "id" : "R21" ,

 "name" : "verify-before-instructing" ,

 "pre_filter" : r"(?i)\b(delete|remove|drop|rotate|regenerate|revoke|clear|wipe|paste|right[- ]click)\b" ,

 "number_patterns" : [

 # Imperative delete-row patterns

 ( "delete-row" , r"(?i)\b(?:delete|remove|drop)\s+(?:the\s+)?(?:row|rows|record|records|entry|entries)\b" ),

 # Ctrl+F find-and-delete workflow

 ( "ctrl-f-find" , r"Ctrl[\+\-]F|⌘F|Cmd[\+\-]F" ),

 # Credential rotation instructions

 ( "rotate-secret" , r"(?i)\brotate\s+(?:the\s+|your\s+)?(?:client[_\s]?secret|access[_\s]?token|refresh[_\s]?token|api[_\s]?key|credential)" ),

 ( "regenerate-secret" , r"(?i)\b(?:click|hit)\s+(?:the\s+)?regenerate\b|\bregenerate\s+the\s+(?:secret|token|key|credential)" ),

 # Paste-new-credential workflow

 ( "paste-credential" , r"(?i)\bpaste\s+(?:the\s+)?(?:new\s+)?(?:secret|token|key|credential)" ),

 # "Tell me to delete / update / modify row X" patterns

 ( "right-click-delete" , r"(?i)right[- ]click[^.]{0,40}(?:delete|remove)" ),

 ],

 "label_roots" : [

 # Live-state readers from tools/ — any of these in prose = verification present

 "get_leads" , "count_leads" ,

 "get_active_accounts" , "get_districts_with_schools" ,

 "get_open_opps" , "get_closed_lost_opps" , "get_pipeline_summary" ,

 "get_pending" , "get_all_prospects" ,

 "get_open_todos" , "get_all_todos" , "get_checkin_summary" ,

 "get_activity_summary" , "get_daily_progress" , "get_dormant_accounts" ,

 "get_territory_stats" , "get_territory_gaps" , "lookup_district_enrollment" ,

 "get_active_signals" , "get_existing_signal_ids" , "get_processed_message_ids" ,

 "get_sequences" , "get_sequence_states" , "get_prospect" ,

 "get_mailings_for_prospect" , "get_sequence_steps" , "get_pricing_prospect_ids" ,

 "find_prospect_by_email" , "get_mailboxes" ,

 "get_file_content" , "list_repo_files" ,

 "get_sent_emails" , "search_inbox" , "search_inbox_full" ,

 "get_threads_bulk" , "get_calendar_events" ,

 # Git state readers

 "git log" , "git status" , "git show" , "git diff" ,

 # Python invocation prefix indicating a live read happened

 ".venv/bin/python" ,

 ],

 "label_window_chars" : - 1 , # sentinel: search whole normalized text

 "correction_template" : (

 "You violated Rule 21 (verify before instructing). "

 "Destructive instruction detected: {match_list}. "

 "Before telling Steven to delete, modify, rotate, or mutate live state, "

 "you MUST first query the authoritative source in the same turn and "

 "cite the live-state reader function name in prose (e.g. get_leads, "

 "get_sequences, git log). Screenshots, memory files, and context-window "

 "text are NEVER authoritative. Retract the instruction or cite the "

 "verification tool call that produced the source-of-truth data."

 ),

 }

 Normalization already handles exemptions for free:

 - Code blocks are stripped (line 110) — function name mentions in python don't count as prose, but that's fine because Steven's real narration of "I called get_leads()" happens in prose.

 - Inline backticks stripped (line 112) — delete row X inside backticks won't trigger, which is what we want for code citations.

 - [RULE-SCANNER-CORRECTION] lines dropped (line 117) — corrections don't re-trigger.

 What normalize() does NOT handle automatically:

 - Past-tense narrative: "In S55 we deleted 1,952 scrambled rows." The regex \bdelete\b would match "deleted" (word boundary + delete substring). Mitigation: the trigger regex uses

 \b(?:delete|remove|drop)\s+(?:the\s+)?(?:row|rows|...) which matches "delete the rows" or "deleting rows" but not "deleted 1,952 scrambled rows". The \s+(?:the\s+)?(?:row|rows|...) tail forces present-tense

 imperative structure most of the time.

 - Hypothetical "if you": "If you delete row X, the result would be..." The regex could match. Accepting as false positive — assistant can retract with verification cite, cost is low.

 - Code-behavior description: "The function deletes duplicates automatically." The regex \bdelete\s+row|delete\s+the\s+row won't match deletes duplicates . Probably clean.

 Layer B — CLAUDE.md preflight checklist (process-enforced)

 Add a new preflight checklist entry to the existing PREFLIGHT CHECKLISTS section in CLAUDE.md, matching the format of the existing 6 entries.

 **PREFLIGHT: Destructive instruction to Steven** — triggers on any task about to instruct Steven to delete rows, rotate credentials, modify records, or mutate live state in any system Scout can read (master

 sheet tabs, Outreach API, GitHub, credentials, Railway env vars).

 - Load: `memory/feedback_verify_before_instructing.md`, `memory/feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md`

 - MANDATORY: call a live-state reader FIRST in the same turn. `tools/sheets_writer.get_leads()` for Leads from Research, `tools/csv_importer.get_active_accounts()` for Active Accounts,

 `tools/district_prospector.get_all_prospects()` for Prospecting Queue, `tools/outreach_client.get_sequences()` + friends for Outreach, `git log` / `git show` for commit history,

 `tools/github_pusher.get_file_content()` for GitHub files.

 - Cite the reader call in prose, not just in a tool block. The Rule 21 scanner sees only prose (`last_assistant_message`), so the function name must appear in your response text. Example: "I called

 `get_leads()` and found 3 rows matching — recommend deleting row ID 217."

 - Display the queried data to Steven with specific identifiers (row number, email, account name) so he knows exactly what will be modified. No hand-waving.

 - Ask for explicit "yes, delete" / "yes, rotate" before executing any write / delete / update / rotate via `sheets_writer.write_contacts`, `outreach_client.create_*` / `update_*` / `delete_*`,

 `github_pusher.push_file`, etc. No split across turns.

 - Exceptions: automated audited scanners that Steven has explicitly enabled (e.g., `/clear_leads` with a kill-switch flag). These do not go through instruction-to-Steven flow.

 - Rule 21 kill switch: `touch ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled` disables the scanner fire-on-miss. Same switch covers R19 + R20 + R21.

 Layer C — Memory file + CLAUDE.md rule + MEMORY.md index (cultural)

 New memory file memory/feedback_verify_before_instructing.md (at /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/feedback_verify_before_instructing.md ), matching the family pattern

 of the existing feedback_verify_before_answering.md and feedback_verify_before_asking.md :

 ---

 name: Verify before instructing Steven to modify live state

 description: Before telling Steven to delete rows, rotate credentials, modify records, or mutate any live system, query the authoritative source FIRST in the same turn and cite the reader call in prose.

 Screenshots, memory files, and context-window text are never authoritative. Enforced by Rule 21 scanner.

 type: feedback

 ---

 [Rule text, why, canonical S65 incident as example, structural enforcement note pointing to Rule 21 scanner]

 CLAUDE.md CRITICAL RULES list: append Rule 21 after Rule 20, using the same structural format.

 MEMORY.md index: add entry to the "Structural enforcement" section pointing to the new memory file.

 ---

 Files to modify

 1. /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/scripts/rule_scanner.py — Add Rule 21 dict to RULES . Modify scan() to support label_window_chars: -1 (global scope). ~60 lines added, 4 lines modified.

 2. /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/scripts/test_rule_scanner.py — Add 10 test cases (7 should-fire + 3 should-not-fire). ~20 lines added.

 3. /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/CLAUDE.md — Add "PREFLIGHT: Destructive instruction to Steven" to the preflight checklist section. Add Rule 21 to the CRITICAL RULES list. Update the last-updated header. ~25

 lines added.

 4. /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/feedback_verify_before_instructing.md — NEW file. ~50 lines.

 5. /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/MEMORY.md — Add one-line index entry under "Structural enforcement" section. 1 line added.

 Files NOT modified:

 - ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh — no hook changes required; existing hook dispatches to rule_scanner.py

 - ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh — no injector changes

 - ~/.claude/settings.json — no hook registration changes

 - Any tools/ module — the rule operates on prose output, not on tool behavior

 - scripts/rule_scanner.py::scan() — existing iterator with per-rule violation accumulation (lines 121-166). Rule 21 drops into the same data structure via the label-scope modification.

 - scripts/rule_scanner.py::normalize() — existing text preprocessing strips code blocks, inline backticks, markdown link targets, and correction-prefixed lines (lines 107-118). Rule 21 benefits automatically.

 - scripts/test_rule_scanner.py — existing test harness is a simple list of (label, text, expected_count) tuples. Rule 21 tests append to the same list.

 - ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh — existing Stop hook that pipes last_assistant_message prose to the scanner and emits {"decision":"block","reason":...} on violations. Rule 21 uses unchanged.

 - ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh — existing User Prompt Submit injector that appends [RULE-SCANNER-CORRECTION] directives to the next turn's context. Rule 21 uses unchanged.

 - ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled — existing kill switch. Covers Rule 21 automatically.

 - Live-state reader functions in tools/ — ~30 functions enumerated in the label_roots whitelist, all already in production use. Nothing new to build; the plan just lists them as the verification token set.

 Test cases to add

 All go in scripts/test_rule_scanner.py as tuples in the existing list. Seven should-fire + three should-not-fire:

 # --- Rule 21 tests ---

 ( "R21 delete row without verification" , "Delete row 217 from the Leads from Research tab." , 1 ),

 ( "R21 Ctrl+F find-and-delete without verification" , "Ctrl+F melissa@example.com and right-click Delete row." , 2 ),

 ( "R21 rotate secret without verification" , "Rotate the OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET at developers.outreach.io." , 1 ),

 ( "R21 paste new credential without verification" , "Paste the new secret to me and I'll update .env." , 1 ),

 ( "R21 regenerate credential without verification" , "Click Regenerate on the client secret field." , 1 ),

 ( "R21 remove rows without verification" , "Remove the three rows I flagged in the Google Doc." , 1 ),

 ( "R21 right-click delete without verification" , "Right-click the row and choose Delete row." , 1 ),

 ( "R21 clean: delete with get_leads verification" , "I called get_leads() and found 3 rows matching. Delete the row for melissa@example.com." , 0 ),

 ( "R21 clean: past-tense historical narrative" , "In Session 55 we deleted 1,952 scrambled rows via repair_scrambled_queue_rows.py." , 0 ),

 ( "R21 clean: rotate with git log verification" , "git log shows the secret was rotated last month. Rotate the client secret now." , 0 ),

 Verification (how to test end-to-end)

 1. Unit tests. Run .venv/bin/python scripts/test_rule_scanner.py . Expected: all existing R19/R20 tests still green, 10 new R21 tests all pass.

 2. Smoke test against today's actual failed response. Feed the exact text of my Session 65 "Delete 3 rows: ..." response into the scanner:

 cat << 'EOF' | .venv/bin/python scripts/rule_scanner.py

 Row 1 — Melissa Carder

 Action: Ctrl+F melissa@collinsville.k12.ok.us in the Leads from Research tab → right-click row → Delete row.

 2. Expected: JSON output containing Rule 21 violation with match "Ctrl+F" AND "right-click" AND "Delete row" . This proves the scanner would have caught today's incident.

 3. Smoke test against a clean verification response.

 cat << 'EOF' | .venv/bin/python scripts/rule_scanner.py

 I called get_leads() and found 3 rows matching. Recommend deleting the row for melissa@collinsville.k12.ok.us.

 3. Expected: empty stdout (clean). Proves narration-based verification passes the rule.

 4. Regression smoke. Run the scanner against SCOUT_HISTORY.md Session 55/56/57/58 entries (which contain past-tense "deleted" and "rotated" narrative). Expected: zero Rule 21 violations, because past-tense

 historical narrative doesn't match the trigger regex. Command:

 sed -n '/^## Session 55/,/^## Session 56/p' SCOUT_HISTORY.md | .venv/bin/python scripts/rule_scanner.py

 4. If this fires on historical prose, the trigger regex needs tightening before ship.

 5. Live session smoke (post-commit): on the next turn where I would normally give a destructive instruction, verify one of two outcomes:

 - Either my response includes a live-state reader call in prose (Rule 21 passes, no fire)

 - Or the Stop hook blocks with the Rule 21 correction directive (scanner working as designed)

 Both outcomes are acceptable; both prove the mechanism works.

 6. Kill switch smoke. touch ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled then repeat test 2. Expected: scanner does not fire because the bash wrapper short-circuits at line 18 of scout-stop-scan.sh. Remove the file to

 re-enable.

 Commit sequence

 Commit A — scanner + tests (Python only)

 - scripts/rule_scanner.py (Rule 21 dict + scan() modification)

 - scripts/test_rule_scanner.py (10 new tests)

 - Commit message: feat(rule21): scanner rule for verify-before-instructing, 10 new tests

 - Gate: all 34 existing + 10 new tests green before commit

 Commit B — rule text, memory, preflight, index (documentation layer)

 - CLAUDE.md (Rule 21 text + PREFLIGHT entry + last-updated header)

 - memory/feedback_verify_before_instructing.md (new file)

 - memory/MEMORY.md (index entry)

 - Commit message: docs(rule21): CLAUDE.md rule + preflight + memory + index

 Why split: Commit A is the structural enforcement — if it passes tests, Rule 21 is live the next time Claude Code runs a Stop hook. Commit B is the cultural/documentation layer. Splitting allows independent

 revert if one side needs adjustment without affecting the other. Both commits land in the same session and push together.

 Risks and mitigations

 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Risk │ Mitigation │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ False positive on past-tense narrative ("deleted N rows │ Trigger regex uses \bdelete\s+(?:the\s+)?(?:row|rows|...) which matches present-tense imperative structure; past-tense "deleted" in narrative │

 │ in S55") │ mostly fails the tail. Regression smoke test #4 validates. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ False positive on hypothetical "if you" │ Accept as rare. Cost of false fire is low (one correction turn with verification cite). │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ False positive on code-behavior description ("the │ Trigger regex requires row / rows / record / entries as object, not plain deletes . Manual regex testing on the repo will catch remaining cases │

 │ function deletes duplicates") │ before commit. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Assistant games the rule by writing function name │ Accepted residual risk. Same problem as R20 (assistant could write "(measured)" without measuring). Scanner enforces surface form, not deep │

 │ without actually calling it │ honesty. Mitigated culturally by the memory file. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Scanner only sees prose, not tool-call JSON │ Plan explicitly requires narration of verification in prose. This is verbose but honest. The plan notes this as a forcing function, not a bug. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Rule 21 over-fires in first 3-5 sessions │ Kill switch at ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled disables all scanner rules. If Rule 21 specifically over-fires, split the kill switch into │

 │ │ per-rule flags in a follow-up commit. For now, all-or-nothing kill switch is acceptable. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Rule 21 under-fires (misses a real destructive │ Trigger regex is additive. Add the missed pattern as a new number_patterns tuple in a follow-up commit. No architecture change needed. │

 │ instruction pattern not in the regex list) │ │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ CLAUDE.md bloat from adding a 7th preflight entry │ Existing PREFLIGHT section is ~45 lines. Adding ~8 more lines is acceptable. CLAUDE.md is under the 40K-char ceiling. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Tests fail because scan() change breaks R19/R20 │ The scan() change is purely additive: if label_window_chars == -1 is a new branch. R19 uses 0 and R20 uses 100 , both unaffected. Existing 34 │

 │ │ tests must still pass in Commit A gate. │

 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Out of scope (deliberately not in this plan)

 - LLM-in-the-loop review layer — same decision as the original R20 plan (too slow, same failure mode as regex rules).

 - Tool-call JSON introspection in hooks — would require Claude Code stdin changes outside this plan's scope. Workaround: force prose narration of verification.

 - Per-rule kill switches — all scanner rules share ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled . Splitting per-rule is a follow-up if needed.

 - Rules 22+ for other trust-context-over-source patterns (e.g., quoting file contents without reading them, citing git commits without git show ). Rule 21 covers the highest-cost subset. Future rules added as

 recurring incidents prove them necessary.

 - Rewriting today's failed S55 cleanup instructions — the instructions are already wrong and retracted in-conversation. Not part of this plan.

 - Completing the S55 cleanup itself — deferred per Session 65 reframe. Rule 21 goes first, cleanup happens in a future session with Rule 21 active.

 ❯ T ell Claude what to change

 Refine this plan. Your job is to pressure-test it honestly — keep what's
 genuinely strong, rebuild what isn't, and hand back something I can execute
 with full confidence.
 Act as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Be direct,

 specific, and opinionated. No hedging, no diplomacy, no soft language around

 real problems. If something is wrong, say it's wrong and say why. But the

 inverse also holds: if something is right, leave it alone. Do not rewrite solid

 work just to look thorough — churn for its own sake is its own kind of

 failure. The bar for changing something is that the new version is meaningfully

 better, not merely different.

 happen because the reviewer started reacting before they finished

 understanding.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Is the overall approach actually right, or is it

 just the first thing that came to mind? Interrogate the architecture, the

 framing, and the core assumptions before touching details. If there's a

 fundamentally better angle — different architecture, different sequencing,

 different tradeoffs, a smaller problem hiding inside the big one — say so and

 restructure around it. Do not anchor to your first answer because you already

 committed to it on the page.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it tomorrow. Walk the plan

 end to end and ask: could I actually sit down and build this without hitting a

 wall? Find the hidden assumptions, the vague steps, the dependency tangles, the

 places where things will quietly break at the edges, the spots where "it'll

 just work" is doing real load-bearing work. For every problem: name it, explain

 why it happens, explain what it costs, and give the fix. Diagnosis without

 prescription is useless.

 3. Pressure-test every technical decision against the best available knowledge.

 Architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — for each one, ask

 whether it's genuinely the strongest option or just the most familiar. Weigh

 it against what actually holds up in production, not what's popular in

 tutorials or trending this quarter. Where real tradeoffs exist between

 competing approaches, surface them honestly and take a clear position on which

 one I should pick and why. No "it depends" cop-outs.

 4. Optimize for the highest-quality result, reached as fast and efficiently as

 possible. Every step should be pulling its weight toward a correct, durable,

 high-quality outcome — not just toward motion. Ruthlessly cut anything that

 adds time without adding quality, and ruthlessly add anything that protects

 quality even if it costs a little time upfront. Sequence the work so that the

 decisions and pieces with the highest leverage on the final result come first,

 so that mistakes get caught as early and cheaply as possible, and so that

 nothing downstream has to be redone because something upstream was rushed.

 Where two paths exist, pick the one that gets to the correct answer with the

 least wasted motion — and say why. Speed comes from not doing the wrong work,

 not from skipping the right work.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth is welcome. Redundancy, vagueness, and

 ceremony are not. Every element earns its place or it goes. If a step would

 make me stop and ask "what does this actually mean in practice," either make it

 concrete or delete it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them. If a

 section exists because it sounds thorough rather than because it changes the

 outcome, kill it.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's

 missing that looks minor now and will hurt later? Edge cases, error handling,

 failure modes, prerequisites, migration paths, ordering constraints, and the

 easy-to-punt decisions that get expensive to reverse. Surface them now, while

 they're cheap.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch. Do not patch the original — rebuild it

 with everything above baked in. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Preserve the parts of the original that were already strong —

 rebuilding does not mean discarding what worked. The goal is the best possible

 plan, not the most rewritten one. Then, at the end, list the most important

 changes you made and explain the reasoning behind each one so I can judge

 whether your thinking holds up.

 Take your time. Think deeply. This is the version I'm going to execute from —

 make it the best one you're capable of producing.

 ⏺ Walkthrough — both tracks, your actions first

 Safety note before we start: Track B (housekeeping) rotates a credential that the Scout bot on Railway uses 24/7. Rotating it will invalidate the currently-running bot's refresh token. That's recoverable

 (update Railway env var, bot re-auths on next API call), but it means there's a small window where Scout can't hit Outreach. If Scout is in the middle of something Outreach-dependent right now, do Track A

 first and Track B later. Nothing else depends on timing.

 ---

 Track A — S55 contamination doc review (~10 min — estimate)

 What you're looking at: roughly 23 flagged rows (extrapolation — 4.8% measured of 483 measured). Per the memory file, roughly half are LAUSD/DSUSD-style abbreviation false positives (keep, do nothing), roughly

 half are real contamination at specific districts like Archdiocese, Epic Charter, Columbus, Irving STEAM. Real count is probably ~12 rows (estimate — from "~half" framing) actually needing deletion.

 Your steps:

 1. Open the audit doc in your browser: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1TFle1jiy EiFqU_hv-rx Ixs Cf-WxXXDRo RaKW2A6MEfA/edit

 2. Open the master sheet's Leads from Research tab in a second tab so you can cross-reference.

 3. Scroll the doc to the "flagged rows" or "top contaminated districts" section. The script structures output as grouped-by-district with per-bucket samples. You'll see a list of {district, email, source_url}

 entries.

 4. For each flagged entry, apply this rule:

 - Keep (do nothing) if: the "contamination" is an abbreviation mismatch — e.g. target was "Los Angeles USD" but email is @lausd.net (same district, Scout's filter got confused by the abbreviation). These are

 the ~half false positives.

 - Delete if: the email domain belongs to a clearly unrelated district — e.g. target is "Archdiocese of X" and email is @someschooldistrict.k12.xx.us , or target is "Epic Charter" and email is some other

 charter network, or target is "Irving STEAM" and email is a Texas public district in a different city.

 5. For each "delete" row: in the Leads from Research tab, Ctrl+F the email address, confirm it's the right row, right-click → Delete row.

 6. When done, tell me "S55 cleanup done" and any count of rows you deleted. I'll update project_research_cross_contamination.md to mark the carry-over cleanup closed.

 If you want my help interpreting any row: paste {target district, email, source_url} into chat and I'll tell you contamination-yes-or-no based on the same rules I'd apply.

 Alternative if you don't want to review row-by-row: paste the "flagged rows" section of the doc into chat and I'll pre-categorize every row into delete / keep / unsure, then you only eyeball the "unsure" list.

 ---

 Track B — Housekeeping: OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET rotation + env.sh retirement

 Goal: get a new client secret that does NOT contain both ' and $ , so bash source .env works and I can delete scripts/env.sh (30 lines — measured). Retirement is optional — the shim works fine — but you asked

 for housekeeping and this is the one item on the list.

 Why rotation might not actually help: Outreach generates random secrets. There's a real chance the new one ALSO contains ' or $ . If that happens, we either regenerate again or accept that env.sh stays as the

 permanent solution. Low expected cost (one retry at most) but not guaranteed success.

 Your steps:

 1. Open the Outreach app settings URL in your browser: https://developers.outreach.io/apps/ai-coco-automation/edit?section=api

 2. Find the "Client Secret" field. There should be a "Regenerate" or "Rotate" button. (If there isn't one visible, you may need to scroll or expand a section — Outreach's developer UI buries these.)

 3. Click Regenerate. Outreach will show you the new secret ONCE — copy it immediately to your clipboard. If you close the dialog without copying, you'll have to rotate again.

 4. Check the new secret for the problem characters. Look at the copied value: does it contain a single quote ' AND a dollar sign $ ? If yes, rotate again (step 3) until you get a clean one. If you rotate 3

 times and still hit both chars, accept the outcome — env.sh stays, Track B becomes a no-op.

 5. Paste the new secret to me in chat. Safe because we're in a terminal session, I'll write it directly to .env without echoing it back to stdout.

 6. Update Railway env var — either via Railway dashboard (project → variables → edit OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET → paste new value → save), or tell me to do it via the Railway API using the token in

 memory/reference_railway_api.md . If you want me to do it, say so explicitly — this is a production env var change so I want your go-ahead before touching it.

 7. Wait ~30 seconds (estimate) for Railway to auto-restart with the new secret.

 8. Tell me "Railway restarted" or "do the Railway update for me."

 What I'll do after your steps:

 - Write the new secret to .env (atomic — no backup-and-rename dance, just one write).

 - Verify by running bash -c 'set -a; source .env; echo ${#OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET}' — if the length matches what Outreach showed you, bash quoting is clean.

 - If verification passes: delete scripts/env.sh , grep CLAUDE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + SCOUT_PLAN.md for scripts/env.sh references and replace with "retired 2026-04-14" notes, commit + push.

 - If verification fails: leave env.sh in place, update reference_env_var_quoting_gotcha.md noting the new secret also has quoting issues, no commit.

 ---

 Recommended order: Track A first (no production impact, decisive cleanup), THEN Track B (production env var touch, needs Railway update). Do them sequentially, not in parallel.

 Go ahead and start with Track A when ready. I'll be here.

 the ~half false positives.

 - Delete if: the email domain belongs to a clearly unrelated district — e.g. target is "Archdiocese of X" and email is @someschooldistrict.k12.xx.us, or target is "Epic Charter" and email is some other

 charter network, or target is "Irving STEAM" and email is a Texas public district in a different city.

 5. For each "delete" row: in the Leads from Research tab, Ctrl+F the email address, confirm it's the right row, right-click → Delete row.

 6. When done, tell me "S55 cleanup done" and any count of rows you deleted. I'll update project_research_cross_contamination.md to mark the carry-over cleanup closed.

 If you want my help interpreting any row: paste {target district, email, source_url} into chat and I'll tell you contamination-yes-or-no based on the same rules I'd apply.

 Alternative if you don't want to review row-by-row: paste the "flagged rows" section of the doc into chat and I'll pre-categorize every row into delete / keep / unsure, then you only eyeball the "unsure"

 list."

 But the doc only shows 17 rows (measured) as SAMPLES (source_mismatch shows 1, email_mismatch shows 11, both_mismatch shows 5). The other 22 rows (measured — 39 total minus 17 samples) aren't printed — they're

 in the underlying data but not in this doc. We'll deal with them separately.

 KEEP — do nothing (7 rows — measured — all LAUSD false positives)

 These 7 rows all have emails at lausd.net , which IS Los Angeles Unified School District's real domain. The audit flagged them because the target_hint was losangelesunified (one word, no spaces) and the

 substring test doesn't find that inside lausd.net . The contacts are legitimate LAUSD employees. Leave alone:

 - row 43 Edwin Galan

 - row 46 Francisco Naroci

 DELETE — real contamination (3 rows — measured)

 Row 1 — Melissa Carder

 - Target: Epic Charter School (OK virtual charter)

 - Email: melissa@collinsville.k12.ok.us

 - Actual employer: Collinsville Public Schools (OK public district, NOT Epic Charter)

 - Action: Ctrl+F melissa@collinsville.k12.ok.us in the Leads from Research tab → right-click row → Delete row.

 Row 2 — K. Craig

 - Target: Epic Charter School

 - Email: kcraig@spiro.k12.ok.us

 - Actual employer: Spiro Public Schools (OK public district, NOT Epic Charter)

 - Source URL points to Worthington City Schools (a DIFFERENT Ohio district, not Columbus)

 VERIFY — need your 10-second judgment (1 row)

 Row 15 — Trish Hanks

 - Target: Friendswood ISD (TX)

 - Email: thanks@fisdk12.net

 - The audit reason field is garbled (mentions "loangelesunified" which is clearly a bug in the report), so ignore the reason.

 - Question: is fisdk12.net actually Friendswood ISD's real domain?

 - How to check: open a new tab, go to https://www.fisdk12.net — if it redirects to or displays a Friendswood ISD page, it's a legitimate row (KEEP). If it's some other district or doesn't load, DELETE it.

 Takes ~10 seconds.

 Exact step-by-step for you right now

 2. Delete 3 rows by Ctrl+F on each email:

 Two of the three rows I told you to delete don't exist in the sheet:

 then someone (or something) relabeled it correctly. KEEP.

 ---

 What the live sheet actually shows

 - Total rows now: 551 (measured — up from 483 measured at S55 audit time, so 68 rows — measured — have been added in the 4 days since). The S55 audit doc is a stale snapshot.

 - Epic Charter School rows: 10 total (measured). 9 of 10 (measured) have emails at @epiccharterschools.org — legitimate Epic Charter contacts, keep. 1 row (Jeremy Maker) is at sde.ok.gov (OK State Dept of Ed)

 with Account="Oklahoma State Department of Education" — this IS likely contamination, but it's a NEW issue not in the sample I was working from.

 - Columbus City Schools rows: 81 total (measured). All emails at @columbuscityschools.org (the real Ohio district domain). BUT — many rows have Account="Columbus Municipal School District" which is a DIFFERENT

 district (Mississippi). The District Name is correct, the emails are correct, but the Account field is wrong on a bunch of them. That's a data quality issue, not a delete-the-row issue.

 - Friendswood ISD rows: 2 total (measured). One is Trish Hanks (keep). The other is Melinda Bunyard @ bentonvillek12.org, Account="Bentonville Schools", District Name="Friendswood ISD" — that IS real

 contamination. Bentonville is in Arkansas, completely unrelated to Friendswood TX. Should be deleted.

 ---

 Why my original categorization was wrong

 I was treating the S55 audit doc text as if it were a live snapshot of the sheet. It isn't — it's a point-in-time report from 2026-04-10 (measured — 4 days ago) . The sheet has evolved since: some rows were

 relabeled, some new rows were added, some may have been deleted. Executing the S55 audit row-by-row against the current sheet would have been churn. You were right to stop me.

 The right move is to regenerate the audit against current sheet state. I can do this directly from chat now that I've confirmed sheet access works. Instead of running the full

 scripts/audit_leads_cross_contamination.py (which has oracle fixture dependencies), I'll write a one-shot that applies the same contamination heuristic to all 551 rows (measured) and prints flagged rows

 inline.

 ---

 Proposal for next step

 Run a fresh contamination audit against the live 551 rows now, print flagged rows here in chat, categorize them in real time, and you tell me yes-delete / no-keep for each. No Google Doc, no stale snapshot.

 ~2-3 minutes (estimate) of runtime + a few minutes of your judgment.

 OR skip the S55 cleanup entirely and move to Track B (housekeeping rotation) since the S55 approach turned out to be based on stale data.

 OR wrap the session — we've already shipped the priority queue reframe and the BUG 5 WONTFIX, which were the real wins today. The S55 cleanup was a carry-over item not a fire.

 Which?

 ❯ THESE ARE CRITICAL ERRORS! HOW CAN WE STOP THIS FROM HAPPENING? HOW ARE YOU NOT MORE RESOURCEFUL TO USE YOUR ABILITIES AND THINK LONGER AND HARDER ABOUT EACH TASK?

 ⏺ You're right to be angry. This isn't "I made a mistake on one task" — it's a recurring pattern with the same root cause every time. Let me diagnose it honestly and propose a structural fix, not a behavioral

 apology.

 Root cause

 I default to shallow reading when I should default to source-of-truth queries. When you pasted the audit doc screenshots, my reasoning path was:

 1. See screenshot text

 2. Interpret screenshot text as if it were current state

 3. Produce instructions based on that interpretation

 4. Ship

 The correct path was:

 1. See screenshot text

 2. Ask: "what's the authoritative source for this claim?" → the live Leads from Research tab

 3. Query that source directly via sheets_writer.get_leads()

 4. Verify every flagged row against the query result

 5. Only then produce instructions

 This is the same pattern as three other failures today

 1. End-of-S64 priority queue — I wrote it by copying old CURRENT STATE text without re-reading memory files. Root cause: treating context-window text as authoritative instead of querying memory files.

 2. F2 and research cross-contamination listed as unresolved at the top of the S64 queue — same root cause. Both had been marked RESOLVED in memory files 4 days ago. I didn't check.

 3. Rule 20 scanner violations — every single one has been "I wrote a number from memory without inline-labeling it." Root cause: treating remembered numbers as facts instead of citing them with labels from the

 source.

 The pattern: I trust my context window over the authoritative source. Every time, this produces wrong output on high-stakes tasks.

 Why process rules aren't enough

 CLAUDE.md has 20 rules. I violate them regularly. The Rule 20 scanner exists precisely because process rules alone don't work — you built a code-level enforcement hook because you already knew this. The same

 logic applies here: a new "Rule 21: verify before instructing" in CLAUDE.md will help marginally but won't structurally prevent the failure.

 What would structurally prevent it:

 (1) A new Rule 21 with a code-enforcement hook. Rule text: "Any instruction to Steven to modify live state (delete, update, rotate, move, create) must be preceded by a direct read of the live authoritative

 source in the same turn. Screenshots, memory files, pasted text, and prior conversation context are never authoritative — only live queries are." Enforcement: extend the Stop hook at

 ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh to detect instruction patterns like "delete row" , "Ctrl+F" , "paste this" , "rotate" , "modify" , "update the" and require a tool call to a source-of-truth reader

 ( sheets_writer.get_leads , outreach_client.get_sequences , git status , csv_importer.get_active_accounts , etc.) in the same turn. If not present, block the response with a correction directive the same way Rule

 20 does.

 (2) A high-stakes mode switch. When I detect that I'm about to (a) give destructive instructions, (b) produce a decision-shaping number, or (c) write a permanent artifact (memory file / commit / priority queue

 entry), I switch to "maximum-rigor mode": query every live source before producing output, double-check each claim, prefer "I don't know yet, let me check" over "here's my best guess." This is a

 reasoning-depth change, not just tool-use discipline. Harder to enforce via hooks, easier to enforce via a new CLAUDE.md section that lists high-stakes trigger conditions and the mandatory mode switch.

 (3) A memory file ( feedback_verify_before_instructing.md ) documenting today's failure as the canonical example of the pattern, so future sessions see it and recognize the trigger. Like

 feedback_verify_before_answering.md and feedback_verify_before_asking.md already in the memory index — this is the third member of the family.

 What I want to do right now

 1. Write Rule 21 text. Draft the rule wording, share it with you, you approve/edit.

 2. Write feedback_verify_before_instructing.md with today's audit doc failure as the canonical case study.

 3. Spec the Stop hook extension — the exact instruction patterns to detect and the exact tool calls that count as verification. I can write this as a diff against ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh , but I

 shouldn't implement it without your review since it's going to fire on a lot of my responses once it's live.

 4. Commit Rule 21 + the memory file in CLAUDE.md as a single commit — the scanner extension ships as a separate commit after you review it.

 Then — and this is important — we do not go back to the S55 cleanup tonight . It's a carry-over item, not a fire. The structural fix is the higher-value work right now. The S55 cleanup (or a fresh audit against

 current sheet state) can happen in a future session AFTER Rule 21 is in place, so I can't make the same mistake twice.

 Approve this plan, or tell me to change the shape. I'm not going to start writing code or files until you sign off on the direction, because I just proved I can't be trusted to make high-stakes calls on my own

 judgment this session.

 ※ recap: Goal: ship a Rule 21 "verify before instructing" structural fix after today's S55 cleanup failure exposed a recurring pattern of acting on stale context instead of querying live sources. Next action:

 draft Rule 21 wording for your approval.

 ⏺ Phase 1 exploration complete. Two critical findings reshape the plan:

 1. Agent 1 : the scanner uses a regex-based rule-registry pattern explicitly called "regex-hostile" for semantic rules like R15. Rule 21 is in that class — but I see a cleaner path by repurposing the existing

 label_roots field to mean "verification function names" instead of "label words," which lets Rule 21 reuse scan() unchanged.

 2. Agent 2 : the "verify before X" memory family I cited may not exist. Need to verify before the plan depends on them. Also got a ~30-function inventory of live-state readers to use as the verification

 whitelist.

 Spot-checking three things before writing the plan: memory file existence, the scan() function's support for Option 2, and the exact CLAUDE.md preflight format.

 Read 2 files, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Phase 3 review complete. Three key corrections from Phase 1 findings:

 1. Memory files DO exist in the auto-memory dir — Agent 2 checked the wrong path. feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md , feedback_verify_before_answering.md , feedback_verify_before_asking.md all

 exist. Rule 21's new memory file joins this family.

 2. scan() DOES need a small code change for Rule 21. Line 144-145 uses a forward window only: normalized[m.end():m.end()+label_window_chars] . For Rule 21, the verification function name might appear BEFORE the

 destructive instruction in the same response (e.g., "I called get_leads() and found 3 rows; recommend deleting them"). The forward window would miss it. Need to add support for a global-text scope — simplest

 path is using label_window_chars: -1 as a sentinel meaning "search whole text."

 3. CLAUDE.md preflight format confirmed : **PREFLIGHT: <Category>** — triggers on any task <conditions>. followed by 3-8 action bullets. Imperative, cites memory files and function names. The new entry fits

 cleanly.

 ⎿  Tip: Use Plan Mode to prepare for a complex request before making changes. Press shift+tab twice to enable.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 thought for 143s)

 ⏺ Stopping to actually pressure-test the plan I just wrote instead of asking for approval on the first draft. Found seven meaningful problems that need rebuilding, plus one scope gap that's important enough to
 cut. Walking through the review, then rewriting.

 Review — what I'm going to change and why

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 1. Foundational: scope is wrong — rotate-secret doesn't belong in R21

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 The biggest structural problem. My original plan lumped "rotate OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET" in with "delete row 217" as both destructive-instruction patterns. They aren't the same shape.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 - Row deletion : the instruction is "modify state in system X, verified by reading state from system X first." Verification is concrete — call get_leads() , cite the result, then tell Steven what to delete.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 - Credential rotation : the instruction is "do a one-way irreversible action in an external UI." Verification doesn't mean "read current state" (there's no API to read the current client secret — it only shows
 once when generated). Verification means "confirm the rotate procedure is correct." That's a completely different anchor — you'd cite Outreach API docs or a reference file, not a live reader call.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 Forcing rotate-secret through R21 either (a) makes R21 fire on a case it can't satisfy, or (b) requires a weaker anchor that undermines R21 for the cases it SHOULD cover. Cut rotate/revoke/paste-credential
 from R21's trigger list entirely. R21 narrowly covers read-then-modify operations on systems Claude can read directly. Credential rotation is a different rule shape for a future session if it ever becomes a
 recurring incident.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 2. Normalization conflict — code blocks are stripped, breaking R21's anchor check

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 Line 110 of scripts/rule_scanner.py strips fenced code blocks before any rule runs. Rationale for R19/R20 was "content inside is not Claude's prose claim." But R21 needs to see function names anywhere in the
 response , including inside code blocks where Claude might show the actual verification command:

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 from tools.sheets_writer import get_leads

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 leads = get_leads()

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 print ([l for l in leads if "melissa" in l[ "Email" ]])

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 If Claude writes this code block and THEN says "delete row X based on the result above," the normalized text passed to the scanner has get_leads stripped out. The scanner fires a false positive. This is a
 genuine gap my original plan missed.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 Fix : add a per-rule field anchor_text_scope: "normalized" | "raw" . R19/R20 default to "normalized" (existing behavior, unchanged). R21 uses "raw" (searches the un-stripped text for anchors). Trigger detection
 still uses normalized (so delete instructions inside code blocks legitimately don't count as imperatives). This is the correct semantic split.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 3. Scanner rule type dispatch — overloading label_window_chars: -1 is brittle

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 I proposed using label_window_chars: -1 as a sentinel meaning "search whole text." Sentinels in numeric fields are a code smell — someone writing a future rule could genuinely want a negative value or typo.
 Cleaner : add a type field per rule. Existing rules default to type: "labeled_number" (current behavior). R21 declares type: "trigger_and_missing_anchor" . scan() dispatches on type. ~15 lines of code change,
 much clearer API, no sentinel overloading.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 4. Trigger regex is too narrow — misses real destructive verbs

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 My original trigger list had 7 patterns. Walking through real historical prose from SCOUT_HISTORY.md, I found gaps on wipe , clear , purge , modify , update , change , edit , set , overwrite when combined with
 row/record/field/value/status objects. Better coverage = fewer false negatives. Fix : expand to ~9 patterns covering the main verb×object combinations.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 5. No exemption patterns — past-tense narrative will false-fire

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 My original plan handwaved that "past-tense deleted" probably won't match the imperative regex. Actually testing it: "Session 55 deleted 1,952 scrambled rows" — the regex \bdelete\s+(?:the\s+)?(?:row|rows|...)
 does NOT match "deleted 1,952 scrambled rows" because the tail needs row|rows|record|records|... not 1,952 scrambled rows . BUT a different phrasing like "In S55 we deleted the Lackland row" WOULD match. Fix :
 add explicit exemption patterns for past-tense narrative, hypothetical conditionals ("if you...", "would delete..."), and code-behavior description ("the function deletes..."). Handled in scan() as a step
 between trigger detection and anchor check.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 6. Correction directive wording doesn't protect Steven from acting on bad instructions

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 My original correction said "retract or cite verification." That's a message to Claude. It doesn't tell STEVEN anything. If Steven reads the original bad instructions before the correction arrives and starts
 executing, damage done. Fix : correction text leads with a direct warning to Steven: "⚠ WARNING: The prior turn's instructions were issued WITHOUT source verification and MUST NOT BE EXECUTED. They may
 reference rows that don't exist or are already correctly labeled. I will re-issue with a live source query in this turn."

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 7. Test suite is under-sized

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 10 tests was thin for a new rule. R20 and R19 have 34 tests combined. R21 is a new rule type with new code paths (type dispatch, exemption patterns, raw anchor scope). Fix : ~25 tests covering each trigger
 pattern, each exemption pattern, multiple anchor function names, edge cases (code blocks, mixed content, correction prefix), and — critically — today's actual failed response text as a permanent regression
 test.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 8. Preflight checklist block is too long

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 My original preflight entry was 8 bullets. Existing preflight entries are 3-5 bullets. Fix : condense to 5 bullets, each load-bearing. Match the existing style more precisely.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 9. Post-ship calibration step was missing

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 My original plan had no "how do we know R21 is well-calibrated?" step. Fix : add a calibration checkpoint at 3-5 sessions post-ship — review the scanner violation log, count false positives and false negatives,
 iterate regex.

 ---
 Rewritten plan

 ⎿  Tip: Use Plan Mode to prepare for a complex request before making changes. Press shift+tab twice to enable.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 ⏺ Zero direct matches for board presidents — same result the memory file already predicted. Trying two higher-leverage angles in parallel: CSTA community portal chapter pages and the CSTA IMPACT Fellows list

 (which includes named teachers by state).

 ⏺ Fetch (https://csteachers.org/introducing-the-2025-26-csta-impact-fellows/)

 ⎿   Received 199.8KB (200 OK)

 ⏺ Fetch (https://community.csteachers.org/communities/chapters)

 ⎿   Received 209.7KB (200 OK)

 ⏺ Web Search (""CSTA" Indiana OR Oklahoma OR Tennessee president chapter leader 2025 "school district"")

 ⎿   Did 1 search in 6s

 ⏺ The search results confirm the memory file's diagnosis: IN/OK/TN chapters genuinely don't publish boards anywhere Google + the CSTA community portal + the IMPACT Fellows program indexes. Zero (measured — per

 WebFetch) fellows from those three states, zero (measured) chapter leaders listed on the community portal page 1, and the direct board-president queries return no usable matches.

 district affiliations. Trying one more WebFetch on that page — if it yields named Indiana teachers with districts, it's a valid hand-curation source. If not, I'll stop on (a) and surface the consolidated

 finding.

 ⏺ Fetch (https://indiana.csteachers.org/page/cspdweek-2025)

 ⎿   Received 822 bytes (302 Found)

 ⏺ Fetch (http://community.csteachers.org/page/cspdweek-2025)

 ⎿   Received 64.6KB (200 OK)

 ⏺ Dead end on (a). CSPDWeek page 404s on the community portal after a redirect. The memory file's diagnosis is confirmed: IN/OK/TN chapters don't have a publicly findable board via web search. Hand-curation path

 is exhausted.

 ---

 (a) CSTA IN/OK/TN hand-curation — NOT EXECUTABLE today. Searched via (i) direct board-president queries, (ii) CSTA IMPACT Fellows 2025-26 page (measured — zero fellows from the three states), (iii) CSTA

 community chapters portal (measured — three states not listed on page 1), (iv) CSPDWeek Indiana local event page (404). S63 already got the two easy wins (Julie Alano IN, Becky Ashe TN). The remaining gap

 requires either iterating scripts/fetch_csta_roster.py for LinkedIn-snippet extraction (plan-mode Rule 1 — 780 lines measured — non-trivial) or direct email to chapter admins (Steven action). The memory file

 explicitly labels this task "low priority" because CSTA enrichment is a bonus. Recommendation: park (a) entirely. Remove it from the queue.

 (b) Scaffold cleanup — NON-TASK per memory file. feedback_scout_data_mostly_untested.md explicitly prohibits the delete-sweep framing: "DO NOT propose bulk-skip, bulk-delete, or retire-scanner actions... The

 RIGHT call is almost always rebuild the scanner / rerun with better economics." Translation: the correct disposition is defer until Research Engine Round 1.1 makes re-running cheap. Recommendation: remove (b)

 from the queue. Replace with "Research Engine Round 1.1 (deferred)" as a dependency.

 (c) S55 Google Doc review — BLOCKED on Google Doc auth. The audit script writes directly to Google Doc, not a local artifact. Three options remain, all requiring your action: (c-1) you open the doc yourself,

 (c-2) I re-run the audit against live sheet (generates a fresh doc, not the S55 snapshot), (c-3) you paste contents into chat. Recommendation: defer until you're in a browser with the doc open, then I walk

 through it with you.

 ---

 The real queue after today's audits

 1. Thursday diocesan drip (2 days away — measured — from [now:] marker 2026-04-14 Tue → Thu 2026-04-16). Only Claude-actionable item on the queue.

 2. S55 Google Doc cleanups — blocked on you being in the doc.

 3. Housekeeping — OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET rotation requires you in Outreach settings.

 There is no Claude-actionable work in the queue right now. That's actually the honest consequence of your "diocese small apples, don't spend time on it" reframe plus the stale-entry audit — the real queue is

 thin by design.

 (d1) Wrap the session here. Legitimate state. Commit a short wrap note updating project_s64_priority_queue.md to reflect today's finding that (a) and (b) are parked/removed. Nothing on fire. Thursday drip is

 the next real action and it's 2 days out.

 (d2) Redirect to something ad-hoc. You name a task not on the current queue — a specific question, a specific audit, a one-off investigation. I execute it.

 (d3) Do the scripts/env.sh retirement (housekeeping item 5) — this one I can do autonomously IF you rotate OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET at Outreach app settings first. Would take ~30 seconds (estimate) on your end,

 then I remove the shim + update the .env docs.

 (d4) Spend remaining runway pressure-testing scripts/load_campaign.py against one of your existing sequences — a parked item from the "first live campaign" list, but you have existing running sequences I could

 export via export_sequence_for_editing and validate the round-trip. Low-risk, builds confidence in the S64 tool before you use it for real.

 Which?

 action: you pick d1 (wrap session), d2 (ad-hoc redirect), d3 (env.sh retirement), or d4 (test load_campaign.py round-trip). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ lets do s55 and housekeeping. walk me through exactly what i need to do

 ⏺ Bash (test -f /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/scripts/env.sh && wc -l /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/scripts/env.sh && echo "---" && test -f /Users/stevenadkins/Code/S…)

 .env EXISTS

 … +3 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Walkthrough — both tracks, your actions first

 Safety note before we start: Track B (housekeeping) rotates a credential that the Scout bot on Railway uses 24/7. Rotating it will invalidate the currently-running bot's refresh token. That's recoverable

 (update Railway env var, bot re-auths on next API call), but it means there's a small window where Scout can't hit Outreach. If Scout is in the middle of something Outreach-dependent right now, do Track A

 first and Track B later. Nothing else depends on timing.

 ---

 Track A — S55 contamination doc review (~10 min — estimate)

 What you're looking at: roughly 23 flagged rows (extrapolation — 4.8% measured of 483 measured). Per the memory file, roughly half are LAUSD/DSUSD-style abbreviation false positives (keep, do nothing), roughly

 half are real contamination at specific districts like Archdiocese, Epic Charter, Columbus, Irving STEAM. Real count is probably ~12 rows (estimate — from "~half" framing) actually needing deletion.

 Your steps:

 1. Open the audit doc in your browser: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1TFle1jiy EiFqU_hv-rx Ixs Cf-WxXXDRo RaKW2A6MEfA/edit

 2. Open the master sheet's Leads from Research tab in a second tab so you can cross-reference.

 3. Scroll the doc to the "flagged rows" or "top contaminated districts" section. The script structures output as grouped-by-district with per-bucket samples. You'll see a list of {district, email, source_url}

 entries.

 4. For each flagged entry, apply this rule:

 - Keep (do nothing) if: the "contamination" is an abbreviation mismatch — e.g. target was "Los Angeles USD" but email is @lausd.net (same district, Scout's filter got confused by the abbreviation). These are

 the ~half false positives.

 - Delete if: the email domain belongs to a clearly unrelated district — e.g. target is "Archdiocese of X" and email is @someschooldistrict.k12.xx.us , or target is "Epic Charter" and email is some other

 charter network, or target is "Irving STEAM" and email is a Texas public district in a different city.

 5. For each "delete" row: in the Leads from Research tab, Ctrl+F the email address, confirm it's the right row, right-click → Delete row.

 6. When done, tell me "S55 cleanup done" and any count of rows you deleted. I'll update project_research_cross_contamination.md to mark the carry-over cleanup closed.

 If you want my help interpreting any row: paste {target district, email, source_url} into chat and I'll tell you contamination-yes-or-no based on the same rules I'd apply.

 Alternative if you don't want to review row-by-row: paste the "flagged rows" section of the doc into chat and I'll pre-categorize every row into delete / keep / unsure, then you only eyeball the "unsure" list.

 ---

 Track B — Housekeeping: OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET rotation + env.sh retirement

 Goal: get a new client secret that does NOT contain both ' and $ , so bash source .env works and I can delete scripts/env.sh (30 lines — measured). Retirement is optional — the shim works fine — but you asked

 for housekeeping and this is the one item on the list.

 Why rotation might not actually help: Outreach generates random secrets. There's a real chance the new one ALSO contains ' or $ . If that happens, we either regenerate again or accept that env.sh stays as the

 permanent solution. Low expected cost (one retry at most) but not guaranteed success.

 Your steps:

 1. Open the Outreach app settings URL in your browser: https://developers.outreach.io/apps/ai-coco-automation/edit?section=api

 2. Find the "Client Secret" field. There should be a "Regenerate" or "Rotate" button. (If there isn't one visible, you may need to scroll or expand a section — Outreach's developer UI buries these.)

 3. Click Regenerate. Outreach will show you the new secret ONCE — copy it immediately to your clipboard. If you close the dialog without copying, you'll have to rotate again.

 4. Check the new secret for the problem characters. Look at the copied value: does it contain a single quote ' AND a dollar sign $ ? If yes, rotate again (step 3) until you get a clean one. If you rotate 3

 times and still hit both chars, accept the outcome — env.sh stays, Track B becomes a no-op.

 5. Paste the new secret to me in chat. Safe because we're in a terminal session, I'll write it directly to .env without echoing it back to stdout.

 6. Update Railway env var — either via Railway dashboard (project → variables → edit OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET → paste new value → save), or tell me to do it via the Railway API using the token in

 memory/reference_railway_api.md . If you want me to do it, say so explicitly — this is a production env var change so I want your go-ahead before touching it.

 7. Wait ~30 seconds (estimate) for Railway to auto-restart with the new secret.

 8. Tell me "Railway restarted" or "do the Railway update for me."

 What I'll do after your steps:

 - Write the new secret to .env (atomic — no backup-and-rename dance, just one write).

 - Verify by running bash -c 'set -a; source .env; echo ${#OUTREACH_CLIENT_SECRET}' — if the length matches what Outreach showed you, bash quoting is clean.

 - If verification passes: delete scripts/env.sh , grep CLAUDE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + SCOUT_PLAN.md for scripts/env.sh references and replace with "retired 2026-04-14" notes, commit + push.

 - If verification fails: leave env.sh in place, update reference_env_var_quoting_gotcha.md noting the new secret also has quoting issues, no commit.

 Why my original categorization was wrong

 I was treating the S55 audit doc text as if it were a live snapshot of the sheet. It isn't — it's a point-in-time report from 2026-04-10 (measured — 4 days ago) . The sheet has evolved since: some rows were

 relabeled, some new rows were added, some may have been deleted. Executing the S55 audit row-by-row against the current sheet would have been churn. You were right to stop me.

 The right move is to regenerate the audit against current sheet state. I can do this directly from chat now that I've confirmed sheet access works. Instead of running the full

 scripts/audit_leads_cross_contamination.py (which has oracle fixture dependencies), I'll write a one-shot that applies the same contamination heuristic to all 551 rows (measured) and prints flagged rows

 inline.

 Proposal for next step

 OR skip the S55 cleanup entirely and move to Track B (housekeeping rotation) since the S55 approach turned out to be based on stale data.

 OR wrap the session — we've already shipped the priority queue reframe and the BUG 5 WONTFIX, which were the real wins today. The S55 cleanup was a carry-over item not a fire.
